turns.user_message: Honestly, I'm not really sure why I'm here. I mean, I know I should quit, but it's just so hard. I've tried before, and it didn't work out.
step2_rag_chunks: If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.
Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the ASSESSMENT phase. Focus on gathering basic facts: how long they have been smoking, how much they smoke per day, and what substance. Do NOT ask about triggers or suggest strategies yet. Ask only one simple factual question.

- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal

turns.user_message: Well, the first time I tried to quit, I used nicotine gum, and it was okay for a few months. But then I started smoking again. I'm a single mom, and I barely make ends meet as it is.
step2_rag_chunks: When a patient has tried to quit multiple times and keeps relapsing, or reports strong physical cravings that are hard to manage, nicotine replacement therapy such as nicotine gum or patches is worth discussing as an additional support tool alongside behavioral strategies.
Nicotine addiction is both physical and psychological. Addressing both aspects can improve success in quitting.

Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.

step2_rag_chunks: Stress is a common trigger for smoking. Relaxation techniques such as deep breathing, meditation, or short walks can help manage stress without using tobacco.
Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.
Keeping your hands busy with activities such as drawing, writing, or using a stress ball can help reduce the urge to smoke.
